editor_settings.title: Fujifilm X100VI vs X100V: Is the Upgrade Worth It?
editor_settings.content:
Fujifilm X100VI vs X100V: Is the Upgrade Worth It?

The X100VI is better. That part is easy.

The harder question is whether it is better enough for you to care.

Quick Verdict

If you are buying into the X100 line for the first time, the X100VI is the one to get. It adds a 40.2MP sensor, in-body image stabilization, 20 film simulation modes, and 6.2K video, which makes it feel like the more complete version of the idea.

If you already own an X100V and love it, the upgrade is only clearly worth it if you want IBIS, more crop room, or better video features. Otherwise, the basic soul of the camera is still very similar.

What Stayed the Same

The most important thing is that Fujifilm did not ruin the formula.

Both cameras center around a fixed 23mm f/2 lens with a 35mm equivalent field of view. Both are built around the X100 idea of tactile dials, compact carry, and shooting that feels more deliberate than a typical modern camera.

If you loved the X100V, the X100VI still feels like home.

What Got Better on the X100VI

This is where the upgrade case starts.

  • X100VI uses a 40.2MP sensor, while X100V uses a 26.1MP sensor
  • X100VI is the first X100 camera with in-body image stabilization
  • X100VI gives you 20 film simulation modes, including REALA ACE
  • X100VI shoots 6.2K video, while X100V tops out at 4K up to 30fps

Those are not fake paper upgrades. They matter if you crop a lot, shoot in low light, or want one camera that handles both stills and stronger video work.

Why Some X100V Owners Still Should Not Upgrade

If you mainly use the X100V for casual stills, travel, and everyday JPEG shooting, you may not feel the jump as strongly as the internet suggests.

The lens feel is the same basic idea. The framing is the same. The charm is the same. The X100VI is more polished, but it is not a completely different life experience.

That is why some X100V owners can happily keep shooting instead of panic-upgrading.

The Price Makes the Decision Harsher

This is where the VI becomes harder to recommend as an automatic upgrade. In 2026, the X100VI is still sitting around $1,799 new and still flirting with backorder territory. That means you are not just paying for better specs. You are paying through a hype-heavy market.

If you want the VI but do not want to play the full retail game, this kind of used listing is the smarter angle:

[product-card:fujifilm-x100vi-silver-camera-used-with-accessories-premium-compact-camera]


Best Upgrade Cases

Upgrade from the X100V if:

  • you need IBIS
  • you crop heavily and want 40.2MP room
  • you care about 6.2K video and newer processing
  • you are already deeply committed to the X100 way of shooting

Keep the X100V if:

  • your current camera already makes you happy
  • you mostly shoot stills in good light
  • you do not need the extra resolution or stabilization

Final Verdict

The Fujifilm X100VI is the better camera. The Fujifilm X100V is still the better value if you already own it and do not need the new hardware.

So yes, the upgrade can be worth it. It just is not automatically worth it.

editor_settings.author: Adrian Brooks
editor_settings.meta_description: The Fujifilm X100VI adds real upgrades over the X100V, but not every X100V owner should rush to switch. Here is the honest call.
editor_settings.meta_keywords: fujifilm x100vi vs x100v, x100vi vs x100v, fujifilm x100v vs x100vi, is x100vi worth upgrading from x100v, fuji x100vi comparison
